learning_objectives: Define public health informatics and its distinction from clinical informatics
Identify the 10 essential public health services and their informatics needs
Understand the public health data ecosystem
readings: Public Health Informatics and Information Systems Ch. 1-2
CDC Public Health Informatics Workforce Competencies

lecture_notes.introduction: Public health informatics applies information science to population-level health challenges. Unlike clinical informatics which focuses on individual patient care, public health informatics deals with surveillance, outbreak detection, and population health assessment.
key_concepts: Public health informatics vs. clinical informatics: population vs. individual focus
10 essential public health services and informatics support for each
Public health data sources: vital records, notifiable diseases, registries, surveys, environmental data
Key organizations: CDC, state/local health departments, WHO, ASTHO
Public health informatics infrastructure: outdated systems and modernization challenges
lecture_notes.demonstration: Tour of CDC data systems: NNDSS, BioSense, NHSN, and their role in national health surveillance
setup_requirements: Access to CDC WONDER and data visualization tools

review.value: The systematic application of information and computer science to public health practice, research, and learning
National Notifiable Diseases Surveillance System: CDC system receiving reports of legally notifiable conditions from state/territorial health departments

learning_objectives: Describe the architecture of disease surveillance systems
Differentiate between passive, active, and syndromic surveillance
Evaluate the role of electronic case reporting in modern surveillance

lecture_notes.introduction: Disease surveillance is the cornerstone of public health practice. Timely, accurate surveillance data drives outbreak detection, resource allocation, and policy decisions.
key_concepts: Passive surveillance: providers report notifiable conditions to health departments
Active surveillance: health departments actively seek cases through outreach
Syndromic surveillance: monitoring pre-diagnostic data (ED chief complaints) for early outbreak signals
Electronic Case Reporting (eCR): automated EHR-to-public-health reporting via FHIR
Surveillance data quality: timeliness, completeness, representativeness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of the AIMS (Association of Public Health Laboratories) electronic lab reporting system and eCR workflow from EHR to health department

review.value: Automated, trigger-based reporting from EHRs to public health using FHIR resources, replacing manual fax/phone reporting
Near real-time monitoring of pre-diagnostic data (ED visits, OTC medication sales) to detect disease outbreaks before laboratory confirmation

learning_objectives: Apply informatics tools to outbreak investigation workflows
Use epidemiological curves and geospatial analysis for outbreak characterization
Understand contact tracing systems and their informatics requirements
readings: CDC Field Epidemiology Manual: Outbreak Investigation Steps

lecture_notes.introduction: Outbreak investigation combines epidemiological methods with informatics tools. COVID-19 revealed both the power and the limitations of public health informatics infrastructure.
key_concepts: Outbreak investigation steps: confirm, define case, find cases, orient (time/place/person), hypothesize, test, implement control
Epi curves: construction, interpretation, propagated vs. point source patterns
Geospatial analysis: GIS mapping of cases, cluster detection, hotspot identification
Contact tracing systems: digital tools, privacy considerations, effectiveness factors
Lessons from COVID-19: data fragmentation, reporting delays, informatics failures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build an epidemiological curve and GIS case map using a simulated foodborne outbreak dataset in Python
setup_requirements: Simulated outbreak dataset with dates and geocoded locations

review.value: Histogram showing case counts over time, used to identify outbreak pattern (point source, propagated, continuous), estimate incubation period, and assess intervention impact
Systematic identification and monitoring of individuals exposed to infectious cases - digital tools can automate notification but adoption depends on trust and privacy

learning_objectives: Describe the architecture and functions of immunization information systems (IIS)
Understand vaccine data exchange standards (HL7 v2 VXU messages)
Evaluate the role of IIS in pandemic response and vaccine equity
readings: CDC Immunization Information System (IIS) Functional Standards

lecture_notes.introduction: Immunization registries are among the most successful public health informatics systems. They track vaccinations across providers, support clinical decision support for vaccine schedules, and were essential infrastructure during COVID-19 vaccination campaigns.
key_concepts: IIS core functions: consolidate records, evaluate schedules, generate reminders, support assessment
HL7 v2 VXU message: vaccine event reporting from EHR/pharmacy to IIS
Bidirectional exchange: query IIS from EHR to retrieve patient immunization history
COVID-19 lessons: mass vaccination data management, equity tracking, federal reporting
IIS modernization: FHIR-based exchange, improved data quality, real-time dashboards
lecture_notes.demonstration: Trace a vaccine administration from clinical encounter through HL7 VXU message to IIS, and query the IIS to retrieve a consolidated immunization record

review.value: Immunization Information System (registry): population-based database consolidating vaccination records from multiple providers to support clinical and public health functions
Vaccine Update message: HL7 v2 message type used to report administered vaccines from providers to the immunization registry

learning_objectives: Analyze the CDC Data Modernization Initiative goals and progress
Evaluate cloud-based approaches to public health data infrastructure
Understand interoperability challenges between clinical and public health systems
readings: CDC Data Modernization Initiative Strategic Plan

lecture_notes.introduction: COVID-19 exposed critical weaknesses in public health data infrastructure: fax-based reporting, fragmented systems, and inadequate analytics capacity. The Data Modernization Initiative aims to fix this.
key_concepts: DMI pillars: point-of-care data, integrated surveillance, workforce, forecasting/analytics, partnerships
Legacy infrastructure problems: siloed systems, manual data entry, delayed reporting
Cloud modernization: scalable infrastructure, real-time data pipelines, shared platforms
FHIR for public health: eCR, lab reporting, immunization reporting via modern APIs
Workforce development: building public health informatics and data science capacity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare a legacy surveillance workflow (fax-based) with a modernized FHIR-based eCR workflow, analyzing time savings and data quality improvements

review.value: CDC-led effort to transform public health data infrastructure from fragmented, legacy systems to integrated, cloud-based, real-time platforms
Automated case reporting using FHIR resources (eICR) transmitted from EHRs to public health authorities, replacing manual reporting processes

class_schedule.topic: Global Health Informatics and One Health
learning_objectives: Describe informatics challenges in global health contexts
Understand the One Health approach linking human, animal, and environmental surveillance
Evaluate international standards and WHO digital health initiatives
readings: WHO Global Strategy on Digital Health 2020-2025

lecture_notes.introduction: Public health informatics operates globally. Pandemics do not respect borders, and informatics solutions must work across vastly different resource settings and information environments.
key_concepts: Global health informatics challenges: infrastructure, connectivity, workforce, standards
DHIS2: the world's largest health information system platform used in 70+ countries
One Health surveillance: integrating human, animal, and environmental data for zoonotic disease detection
WHO digital health standards and SMART Guidelines for clinical recommendations
International Health Regulations (IHR) and global disease notification requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Explore DHIS2 demo instance showing disease surveillance dashboards from a low-resource setting

review.value: District Health Information Software 2: open-source health information platform used by 70+ countries for routine health data collection, analysis, and visualization
Collaborative approach recognizing the interconnection of human, animal, and environmental health - critical for detecting zoonotic disease outbreaks early

questions.question: Compare passive surveillance, active surveillance, and syndromic surveillance. For a novel respiratory virus outbreak, explain which surveillance approach would detect it earliest and why.
questions.answer: Passive surveillance relies on providers voluntarily reporting known notifiable conditions - it would be slowest because the novel virus may not yet be a reportable condition. Active surveillance involves health departments actively seeking cases by contacting labs and hospitals - faster but resource-intensive and dependent on knowing what to look for. Syndromic surveillance monitors pre-diagnostic signals like ED chief complaints ('fever + cough') and is the earliest detector because it identifies unusual patterns before diagnoses are confirmed. For a novel respiratory virus, syndromic surveillance would detect it earliest by identifying an unexpected spike in respiratory chief complaints in ED data, triggering investigation before the pathogen is even identified.

rubric.key: Correct identification of syndromic as earliest detector
Clear reasoning for why syndromic detects earliest

questions.question: Explain how Electronic Case Reporting (eCR) works from a technical perspective. What triggers a report, what standard is used, and how does it improve upon manual reporting?
questions.answer: eCR workflow: (1) A trigger event occurs in the EHR - a diagnosis code, lab result, or medication order matching a reportable condition trigger code set (e.g., positive COVID-19 PCR). (2) The EHR generates an electronic Initial Case Report (eICR) in FHIR/CDA format containing patient demographics, clinical data, and the triggering condition. (3) The eICR is transmitted to a decision support platform (APHL AIMS) that determines which jurisdiction(s) should receive the report. (4) Relevant reports are routed to the appropriate state/local health department's surveillance system. Improvements over manual: eliminates provider burden of filling out forms, reduces reporting delays from days/weeks to near real-time, improves completeness by automating trigger detection, standardizes data format reducing data entry errors, and includes richer clinical context than manual forms.

rubric.key: Improvements over manual reporting clearly stated

questions.question: Describe three key failures in U.S. public health data infrastructure exposed by the COVID-19 pandemic, and for each, explain what the CDC Data Modernization Initiative proposes to address it.
questions.answer: (1) Fax-based disease reporting: Many jurisdictions received COVID case reports by fax, causing days-long delays in case counts. DMI response: implement eCR and electronic lab reporting via FHIR APIs for near real-time automated reporting. (2) Fragmented, siloed surveillance systems: Each disease program operated its own data system with no integration, making it impossible to link COVID testing, cases, hospitalizations, and deaths. DMI response: build integrated surveillance platforms with shared data models and cloud infrastructure enabling cross-program analytics. (3) Inadequate analytics and visualization capacity: Health departments lacked tools and workforce to analyze rapidly evolving pandemic data and communicate it to decision-makers. DMI response: invest in modern BI/analytics platforms and build public health data science workforce through training programs.

rubric.key: Corresponding DMI solutions for each failure

questions.question: Explain how an Immunization Information System (IIS) consolidates vaccine records from multiple providers. Why is this consolidation important for patient care and public health?
questions.answer: Consolidation process: When a provider administers a vaccine, an HL7 VXU message is sent to the IIS containing patient demographics, vaccine type (CVX code), lot number, administration date, and site. The IIS uses matching algorithms (based on name, DOB, gender, address) to link the new record to the patient's existing immunization history, even if the patient received vaccines from different providers (pediatrician, pharmacy, school clinic, public health clinic). Importance for patient care: Clinicians can query the IIS to view a complete vaccination history regardless of where vaccines were given, preventing duplicate vaccinations and identifying missing doses. The IIS can evaluate the record against the CDC recommended schedule and generate clinical decision support for due/overdue vaccines. Importance for public health: IIS data enables population-level assessment of vaccination coverage rates, identification of under-immunized populations for targeted outreach, and rapid vaccine campaign management during outbreaks.

rubric.key: Correct description of record consolidation mechanism

major_assignments.title: Public Health Surveillance System Design
major_assignments.description: Design a modern disease surveillance system for a state health department that addresses the lessons learned from COVID-19. Include surveillance architecture, data standards, analytics capabilities, and equity monitoring.
objectives: Apply public health informatics principles to surveillance system design
Incorporate modern standards (FHIR, eCR) into the architecture
Design analytics dashboards for outbreak detection and response
Include health equity monitoring in surveillance design
deliverables: Data flow diagrams showing reporting from clinical to public health systems
Analytics dashboard mockup for outbreak monitoring
Equity monitoring framework with stratification plan

criteria: Interoperability with clinical and federal systems
Dashboard design is actionable and clear
Surveillance data stratified by RELD categories
Targeted intervention recommendations based on disparities
Clear communication to public health stakeholders
Actionable analytics and outbreak detection capabilities

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of public health informatics concepts and surveillance systems

class_participation.description: Engagement in case studies, outbreak simulations, and class discussions

major_assignments.description: Public health surveillance system design project

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of surveillance, outbreak investigation, and immunization informatics

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including data modernization and global health informatics

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Outbreak simulation exercises are mandatory. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 10% per day up to 5 days. No extensions without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Outbreak investigation exercises are team-based. Written assignments must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All analysis must use properly cited data sources. Public health data must be used responsibly and ethically.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with Python (geopandas, folium), internet access, CDC WONDER access. GIS software recommended.
